title: In-Class Exercise 4: Visual Statistical Analysis with Tableau
text: In-Class Exercise 4 consists of two Tableau dashboards. One exploring English score distributions by class, and another analysing Food Service KPI ratings using diverging stacked bar charts.

title: In-Class Exercise 4: Visual Statistical Analysis with Tableau
text: In-Class Exercise 4 consists of two Tableau dashboards. One exploring English score distributions by class, and another analysing Food Service KPI ratings using diverging stacked bar charts.

title: In-Class Exercise 4: Visual Statistical Analysis with Tableau
section: Dashboard 1 — English Score Distribution by Class
text:
Dashboard 1 — English Score Distribution by Class
This dashboard shows a boxplot of English scores by class alongside a histogram of the overall score distribution. The average English score across all students is 67.18.



English Score Distribution by Class


From the Exam Data, I noticed that 3D performed better than 3C in the English exam, as 3D has a higher overall score distribution. This shows that the boxplot is useful because it allows me to compare not only the general performance between classes, but also the spread and consistency of scores within each class.
The boxplot also helps reduce the zone of irrelevancy. In a bar chart, the viewer may focus too much on small differences between bars that may not be meaningful, making minor gaps look more important than they really are. With a boxplot, I can see whether the scores actually overlap, whether the difference between classes is meaningful, and whether the overall distribution supports the conclusion. This makes the boxplot more useful for comparing exam performance across classes.

title: In-Class Exercise 4: Visual Statistical Analysis with Tableau
text:
Dashboard 2 — Food Service KPI Report
This dashboard uses diverging stacked bar charts to compare food service satisfaction ratings across different wards and months (October and November).



Food Service KPI Report


For the Food Service Survey Data, the dashboards help summarise many responses in a clear and organised way. The charts made it easier to compare satisfaction levels across different food service items, months and wards. I could quickly see which areas received more positive ratings and which areas may need improvement.

title: In-Class Exercise 4: Visual Statistical Analysis with Tableau
text:
Reflections
Overall, I learnt that visual analytics is useful because it turns raw data into insights that are easier to understand, explain and use for decision making. Different chart types serve different purposes — boxplots are better for comparing distributions across groups, while diverging stacked bar charts work well for summarising Likert-scale survey data across multiple categories.

title: In-Class Exercise 2: Superstore Sales and Profit Report
text:
In-Class Exercise 2 is a continuation of In-Class Exercise 1, where we used the Superstore dataset to build a sales and profit dashboard in Tableau Public.
The dashboard visualises annual and monthly sales and profit by region from 2022 to 2025.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text: Visualising uncertainty is about showing not just the point estimate (like a mean) but also how confident we are in that estimate. In this exercise I learnt to plot error bars, confidence intervals and uncertainty using ggdist.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text: Visualising uncertainty is about showing not just the point estimate (like a mean) but also how confident we are in that estimate. In this exercise I learnt to plot error bars, confidence intervals and uncertainty using ggdist.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
Reflections
The difference between standard error bars and confidence intervals was much clearer after seeing them plotted side by side. The 95% CI version (mean ± 1.96 × SE) is more informative than just showing SE.
The interactive version combining plotly and DT was the most practical, being able to hover over a point and see the exact confidence interval values, and then filter the table at the same time, is very useful for presentations.
stat_gradientinterval() from ggdist was visually striking — the gradient effect gives a more intuitive feel for where the uncertainty is concentrated.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
Getting Started

Loading packages

pacman::p_load(plotly, crosstalk, DT,
               ggdist, ggridges, colorspace,
               gganimate, tidyverse)



Importing data

exam <- read_csv("Exam_data.csv")



Computing summary statistics

my_sum <- exam %>%
  group_by(RACE) %>%
  summarise(
    n=n(),
    mean=mean(MATHS),
    sd=sd(MATHS)
    ) %>%
  mutate(se=sd/sqrt(n-1))

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
Visualising Uncertainty — ggplot2 methods

Standard error bars

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(my_sum) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(x=RACE,
        ymin=mean-se,
        ymax=mean+se),
    width=0.2,
    colour="black",
    alpha=0.9,
    linewidth=0.5) +
  geom_point(aes(x=RACE,
                 y=mean),
             stat="identity",
             color="red",
             size = 1.5,
             alpha=1) +
  ggtitle("Standard error of mean maths score by race")






95% Confidence interval

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(my_sum) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(x=reorder(RACE, -mean),
        ymin=mean-1.96*se,
        ymax=mean+1.96*se),
    width=0.2,
    colour="black",
    alpha=0.9,
    linewidth=0.5) +
  geom_point(aes(x=RACE,
                 y=mean),
             stat="identity",
             color="red",
             size = 1.5,
             alpha=1) +
  labs(x = "Maths score",
       title = "95% confidence interval of mean maths score by race")






Interactive error bars — 99% CI

The plotThe code chunk





















shared_df = SharedData$new(my_sum)

bscols(widths = c(4,8),
       ggplotly((ggplot(shared_df) +
                   geom_errorbar(aes(
                     x=reorder(RACE, -mean),
                     ymin=mean-2.58*se,
                     ymax=mean+2.58*se),
                     width=0.2,
                     colour="black",
                     alpha=0.9,
                     size=0.5) +
                   geom_point(aes(
                     x=RACE,
                     y=mean,
                     text = paste("Race:", `RACE`,
                                  "<br>N:", `n`,
                                  "<br>Avg. Scores:", round(mean, digits = 2),
                                  "<br>95% CI:[",
                                  round((mean-2.58*se), digits = 2), ",",
                                  round((mean+2.58*se), digits = 2),"]")),
                     stat="identity",
                     color="red",
                     size = 1.5,
                     alpha=1) +
                   xlab("Race") +
                   ylab("Average Scores") +
                   theme_minimal() +
                   theme(axis.text.x = element_text(
                     angle = 45, vjust = 0.5, hjust=1)) +
                   ggtitle("99% Confidence interval of average maths scores by race")),
                tooltip = "text"),
       DT::datatable(shared_df,
                     rownames = FALSE,
                     class="compact",
                     width="100%",
                     options = list(pageLength = 10,
                                    scrollX=T),
                     colnames = c("No. of pupils",
                                  "Avg Scores",
                                  "Std Dev",
                                  "Std Error")) %>%
         formatRound(columns=c('mean', 'sd', 'se'),
                     digits=2))

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
Visualising Uncertainty — ggdist methods

stat_pointinterval()

The plotThe code chunk














exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE,
             y = MATHS)) +
  stat_pointinterval() +
  labs(title = "Visualising confidence intervals of mean math score",
       subtitle = "Mean Point + Multiple-interval plot")






stat_pointinterval() with .width

The plotThe code chunk














exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE,
             y = MATHS)) +
  stat_pointinterval(.width = 0.95,
                     .point = median,
                     .interval = qi) +
  labs(title = "Visualising confidence intervals of mean math score",
       subtitle = "Mean Point + Multiple-interval plot")






stat_gradientinterval()

The plotThe code chunk














exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE,
             y = MATHS)) +
  stat_gradientinterval(
    fill = "skyblue",
    show.legend = TRUE
  ) +
  labs(title = "Visualising confidence intervals of mean math score",
       subtitle = "Gradient + interval plot")

title: Hands-on Exercise 4a: Visualising Distribution
text: In this exercise I learnt two relatively new statistical graphic methods for visualising distribution — ridgeline plots and raincloud plots — using ggridges and ggdist.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4a: Visualising Distribution
text: In this exercise I learnt two relatively new statistical graphic methods for visualising distribution — ridgeline plots and raincloud plots — using ggridges and ggdist.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4a: Visualising Distribution
text:
Reflections
This exercise introduced two new ways to visualise distribution. Ridgeline plots are great for comparing distributions across many groups at once, much cleaner than stacking multiple histograms.
The raincloud plot was the most interesting, it combines three views in one chart: the density shape, the boxplot summary, and the actual data points. This gives a much more complete picture than just a boxplot alone.
The step-by-step build up of the raincloud plot (half eye → boxplot → dots → flip) was a good way to understand how each layer adds information.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4a: Visualising Distribution
text:
Getting Started

Loading packages

pacman::p_load(ggdist, ggridges, ggthemes,
               colorspace, tidyverse)



Importing data

exam <- read_csv("Exam_data.csv")

title: Hands-on Exercise 4a: Visualising Distribution
text:
Visualising Distribution with Ridgeline Plot
A ridgeline plot (also called a Joyplot) reveals the distribution of a numeric value for several groups. It works best when there are 5 or more groups with a clear pattern or ranking.

Basic ridgeline plot

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = ENGLISH,
           y = CLASS)) +
  geom_density_ridges(
    scale = 3,
    rel_min_height = 0.01,
    bandwidth = 3.4,
    fill = lighten("#7097BB", .3),
    color = "white"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "English grades",
    expand = c(0, 0)
    ) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = NULL, expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))) +
  theme_ridges()






Varying fill colours along the x axis

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = ENGLISH,
           y = CLASS,
           fill = stat(x))) +
  geom_density_ridges_gradient(
    scale = 3,
    rel_min_height = 0.01) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Temp. [F]",
                       option = "C") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "English grades",
    expand = c(0, 0)
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = NULL, expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))) +
  theme_ridges()






Mapping probabilities directly onto colour

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = ENGLISH,
           y = CLASS,
           fill = 0.5 - abs(0.5-stat(ecdf)))) +
  stat_density_ridges(geom = "density_ridges_gradient",
                      calc_ecdf = TRUE) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Tail probability",
                       direction = -1) +
  theme_ridges()






Ridgeline plots with quantile lines

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = ENGLISH,
           y = CLASS,
           fill = factor(stat(quantile))
           )) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf = TRUE,
    quantiles = 4,
    quantile_lines = TRUE) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(name = "Quartiles") +
  theme_ridges()






Ridgeline plot with tail probability cut points

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = ENGLISH,
           y = CLASS,
           fill = factor(stat(quantile))
           )) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf = TRUE,
    quantiles = c(0.025, 0.975)
    ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    name = "Probability",
    values = c("#FF0000A0", "#A0A0A0A0", "#0000FFA0"),
    labels = c("(0, 0.025]", "(0.025, 0.975]", "(0.975, 1]")
  ) +
  theme_ridges()

title: Hands-on Exercise 4a: Visualising Distribution
text:
Visualising Distribution with Raincloud Plot
A raincloud plot combines a half-density plot, a boxplot and a dot plot — giving a much richer picture of the data distribution than a boxplot alone.

Step 1 — Half Eye graph

The plotThe code chunk














exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE,
             y = ENGLISH)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.5,
               justification = -0.2,
               .width = 0,
               point_colour = NA)






Step 2 — Adding boxplot with geom_boxplot()

The plotThe code chunk














exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE,
             y = ENGLISH)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.5,
               justification = -0.2,
               .width = 0,
               point_colour = NA) +
  geom_boxplot(width = .20,
               outlier.shape = NA)






Step 3 — Adding dot plots stat_dots()

The plotThe code chunk














exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE,
             y = ENGLISH)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.5,
               justification = -0.2,
               .width = 0,
               point_colour = NA) +
  geom_boxplot(width = .20,
               outlier.shape = NA) +
  stat_dots(side = "left",
            justification = 1.2,
            binwidth = .5,
            dotsize = 2)






Step 4 — Final raincloud plot

The plotThe code chunk














exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE,
             y = ENGLISH)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.5,
               justification = -0.2,
               .width = 0,
               point_colour = NA) +
  geom_boxplot(width = .20,
               outlier.shape = NA) +
  stat_dots(side = "left",
            justification = 1.2,
            binwidth = .5,
            dotsize = 2) +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_economist()

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
In this exercise, I explored how to make static ggplot2 charts interactive using the ggiraph and plotly packages. Key techniques include:

Adding tooltips to display information on hover
Using data_id to highlight related elements together
Creating onclick effects to link charts to external pages
Building coordinated multiple views where interactions on one chart affect another
Using DT to create searchable, interactive data tables
Using crosstalk to link a chart and a table together




Interactivity makes charts more useful for exploration — users can investigate specific data points without needing a separate table or annotation.




Understanding the difference between tooltip, data_id and onclick arguments in ggiraph
Setting up coordinated views required linking plots through a shared highlight_key()

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
In this exercise, I explored how to make static ggplot2 charts interactive using the ggiraph and plotly packages. Key techniques include:

Adding tooltips to display information on hover
Using data_id to highlight related elements together
Creating onclick effects to link charts to external pages
Building coordinated multiple views where interactions on one chart affect another
Using DT to create searchable, interactive data tables
Using crosstalk to link a chart and a table together




Interactivity makes charts more useful for exploration — users can investigate specific data points without needing a separate table or annotation.




Understanding the difference between tooltip, data_id and onclick arguments in ggiraph
Setting up coordinated views required linking plots through a shared highlight_key()

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
Getting Started

Loading packages

pacman::p_load(ggiraph, plotly, 
               patchwork, DT, 
               crosstalk, tidyverse)



Importing data

exam_data <- read_csv("Exam_data.csv")

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
Interactive Data Visualisation — ggiraph methods

Tooltip effect
Hovering over a data point shows the student’s ID.

The plotThe code chunk











p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = ID),
    stackgroups = TRUE, 
    binwidth = 1, 
    method = "histodot") +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, 
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 6,
  height_svg = 6*0.618
)






Displaying multiple information on tooltip
Hovering shows both the student’s ID and Class.

The plotThe code chunk











exam_data$tooltip <- c(paste0(     
  "Name = ", exam_data$ID,         
  "\n Class = ", exam_data$CLASS)) 

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = exam_data$tooltip), 
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot") +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 8,
  height_svg = 8*0.618
)






Customising tooltip style
Using CSS to change tooltip background and font colour.

The plotThe code chunk











tooltip_css <- "background-color:white;
font-style:bold; color:black;"

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(tooltip = ID),                   
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618,
  options = list(
    opts_tooltip(
      css = tooltip_css))
)






Displaying statistics on tooltip
Tooltip shows the mean Maths score with confidence interval per race.

The plotThe code chunk











tooltip <- function(y, ymax, accuracy = .01) {
  mean <- scales::number(y, accuracy = accuracy)
  sem <- scales::number(ymax - y, accuracy = accuracy)
  paste("Mean maths scores:", mean, "+/-", sem)
}

gg_point <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
                   aes(x = RACE)) +
  stat_summary(aes(y = MATHS, 
                   tooltip = after_stat(  
                     tooltip(y, ymax))),  
    fun.data = "mean_se", 
    geom = GeomInteractiveCol,  
    fill = "light blue"
  ) +
  stat_summary(aes(y = MATHS),
    fun.data = mean_se,
    geom = "errorbar", width = 0.2, size = 0.2
  )

girafe(ggobj = gg_point,
       width_svg = 8,
       height_svg = 8*0.618)






Hover effect with data_id
Elements of the same CLASS are highlighted together on mouse over.

The plotThe code chunk











p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(           
    aes(data_id = CLASS),             
    stackgroups = TRUE,               
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618                      
)






Styling hover effect
Custom CSS changes the highlight colour and dims non-hovered elements.

The plotThe code chunk











p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(data_id = CLASS),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618,
  options = list(                        
    opts_hover(css = "fill: #202020;"),  
    opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.2;") 
  )                                        
)






Combining tooltip and hover effect

The plotThe code chunk











p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(tooltip = CLASS, 
        data_id = CLASS),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618,
  options = list(                        
    opts_hover(css = "fill: #202020;"),  
    opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.2;") 
  )                                        
)






Click effect with onclick
Clicking a data point opens the MOE school finder page.

The plotThe code chunk











exam_data$onclick <- sprintf("window.open(\"%s%s\")",
"https://www.moe.gov.sg/schoolfinder?journey=Primary%20school",
as.character(exam_data$ID))

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(onclick = onclick),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618)






Coordinated Multiple Views with ggiraph
Hovering highlights the same student across both plots simultaneously.

The plotThe code chunk











p1 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(data_id = ID),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)

p2 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(data_id = ID),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)

girafe(code = print(p1 + p2), 
       width_svg = 6,
       height_svg = 3,
       options = list(
         opts_hover(css = "fill: #202020;"),
         opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.2;")
         )
       )

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
Interactive Data Visualisation — plotly methods

Basic interactive scatter plot using plot_ly()

The plotThe code chunk











plot_ly(data = exam_data, 
        x = ~MATHS, 
        y = ~ENGLISH)






With colour by gender

The plotThe code chunk











plot_ly(data = exam_data, 
        x = ~MATHS, 
        y = ~ENGLISH, 
        color = ~GENDER)






Using ggplotly()
Converting a ggplot chart into an interactive plotly chart.

The plotThe code chunk











p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
            aes(x = MATHS,
                y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))
ggplotly(p)






Coordinated Multiple Views with plotly

The plotThe code chunk











d <- highlight_key(exam_data)

p1 <- ggplot(data=d, 
            aes(x = MATHS,
                y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))

p2 <- ggplot(data=d, 
            aes(x = MATHS,
                y = SCIENCE)) +
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))

subplot(ggplotly(p1),
        ggplotly(p2))

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
Interactive Data Table — DT package

The plotThe code chunk











DT::datatable(exam_data, 
              class= "compact")

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
Linked Brushing — crosstalk method

The plotThe code chunk





















d <- highlight_key(exam_data) 
p <- ggplot(d, 
            aes(ENGLISH, 
                MATHS)) + 
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))

gg <- highlight(ggplotly(p),        
                "plotly_selected")  

crosstalk::bscols(gg,               
                  DT::datatable(d), 
                  widths = 5)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1: A Layered Grammar of Graphics
text: In this exercise, I was introduced to ggplot2 and the layered grammar of graphics. I learned that every chart in R is built by adding layers — starting from data, then aesthetics, then geometry. I explored many different chart types including histograms, dot plots, box plots, violin plots, and scatter plots. I also discovered how facets allow us to split charts by categories, and how built-in themes like theme_classic() and theme_minimal() can instantly change the look of a chart. This exercise showed me that ggplot2 is both systematic and flexible.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1: A Layered Grammar of Graphics
text: In this exercise, I was introduced to ggplot2 and the layered grammar of graphics. I learned that every chart in R is built by adding layers — starting from data, then aesthetics, then geometry. I explored many different chart types including histograms, dot plots, box plots, violin plots, and scatter plots. I also discovered how facets allow us to split charts by categories, and how built-in themes like theme_classic() and theme_minimal() can instantly change the look of a chart. This exercise showed me that ggplot2 is both systematic and flexible.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1: A Layered Grammar of Graphics
text:
Install and launching R packages
The code chunk below uses p_load() of pacman package to check if tidyverse packages are installed in the computer. If they are, then they will be launched into R.

pacman::p_load(tidyverse)


exam_data <- read_csv("Exam_data.csv")

title: Hands-on Exercise 1: A Layered Grammar of Graphics
text:
Loading Tidyverse
This code chunk uses pacman’s p_load() to load tidyverse family of packages into R environment.

The plotThe code














ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = RACE)) +
  geom_bar()

title: Hands-on Exercise 1: A Layered Grammar of Graphics
text:
R Graphics histogram

The plotThe code














hist(exam_data$MATHS)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1: A Layered Grammar of Graphics
text:
ggplot2 histogram

The plotThe code














ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=10, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="black", 
                 fill="grey") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")

title: About
text:
About Me
Hi there! Thank you for visiting my page, where I document my learning journey through ISSS608 Visual Analytics and Applications as part of my Master of IT in Business programme at Singapore Management University (SMU). I hope your journey is as fulfilling as mine!
I’m Carina — passionate about turning raw data into clear, meaningful stories through the power of visualisation. When I’m not exploring data, you’ll find me spending time with my cats, Meko and Zeko, or discovering new cafes around Singapore ☕
Special thanks to Prof. Kam Tin Seong for his dedication, patience, and generosity in sharing his knowledge. 🙏

title: About
text:
About This Course
The course draws from research and practice across Data Visualisation, Human-Computer Interaction, Data Analytics, and Usability Engineering. It equips students with the skills to:

Interact with data from various sources and formats using visual analytics techniques
Build interactive and reproducible data visualisation applications
Apply a layered grammar of graphics to communicate data insights effectively
Use tools such as R, ggplot2, Quarto, and Shiny to create compelling visual narratives

This portfolio documents my learning journey through the hands-on exercises, in-class activities, and take-home projects assigned throughout the course.

title: Hands-on Exercise 2: Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text: In this exercise, I explored ggplot2 extensions that go beyond basic charts. I learned how ggrepel prevents overlapping text labels, making scatter plots much cleaner and easier to read. I also experimented with ggthemes and hrbrthemes to apply professional styling, and used patchwork to combine multiple plots into a single layout. These tools showed me that good data visualisation is as much about presentation as it is about the data itself.

title: Hands-on Exercise 2: Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text: In this exercise, I explored ggplot2 extensions that go beyond basic charts. I learned how ggrepel prevents overlapping text labels, making scatter plots much cleaner and easier to read. I also experimented with ggthemes and hrbrthemes to apply professional styling, and used patchwork to combine multiple plots into a single layout. These tools showed me that good data visualisation is as much about presentation as it is about the data itself.

title: Hands-on Exercise 2: Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
Getting Started

Loading the packages

pacman::p_load(ggrepel, patchwork, 
               ggthemes, hrbrthemes,
               tidyverse)



Importing data

exam_data <- read_csv("Exam_data.csv")

title: Hands-on Exercise 2: Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
Beyond ggplot2 Annotation: ggrepel
One of the challenges when annotating scatter plots is that labels often overlap each other. The ggrepel package solves this by automatically adjusting label positions.

Without ggrepel (messy labels)

The plotThe code














ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, linewidth=0.5) +  
  geom_label(aes(label = ID), 
             hjust = .5, 
             vjust = -.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")






With ggrepel (clean labels)

The plotThe code














ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, linewidth=0.5) +  
  geom_label_repel(aes(label = ID), 
                   fontface = "bold") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")

title: Hands-on Exercise 2: Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
Beyond ggplot2 Themes
The ggthemes and hrbrthemes packages provide ready-made professional themes for ggplot2 charts.

Default theme_gray()

The plotThe code














ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  theme_gray() +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")






Using ggthemes: theme_economist()

The plotThe code














ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_economist()






Using hrbrthemes: theme_ipsum()

The plotThe code














ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum()






theme_ipsum() with custom settings

The plotThe code














ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum(axis_title_size = 18,
              base_size = 15,
              grid = "Y")

title: Hands-on Exercise 2: Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
Beyond Single Graph
The patchwork package makes it easy to combine multiple ggplot2 charts into one layout.

Creating the individual plots

p1 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")

p2 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of English scores")

p3 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x= MATHS, 
                 y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, linewidth=0.5) +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")



Combining two graphs

The plotThe code














p1 + p2






Combining three graphs

The plotThe code














(p1 / p2) | p3






With tags

The plotThe code














((p1 / p2) | p3) + 
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = 'I')






With inset

The plotThe code














p3 + inset_element(p2, 
                   left = 0.02, 
                   bottom = 0.7, 
                   right = 0.5, 
                   top = 1)






patchwork + ggtheme combined

The plotThe code














patchwork <- (p1 / p2) | p3
patchwork & theme_economist()

title: Hands-on Exercise 2: Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
Extra Alternatives

Alternative 1: Using theme_wsj()
I used theme_wsj() instead of theme_economist() for a cleaner, more minimal Wall Street Journal style.

The plotThe code














ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, color="grey25", fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_wsj()






Alternative 2: Using theme_fivethirtyeight()
This theme mimics the style used by FiveThirtyEight, great for clean data journalism visuals.

The plotThe code














ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, color="grey25", fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_fivethirtyeight()






Alternative 3: Different patchwork layout
Instead of stacking p1/p2 on the left, I place p1 on top spanning full width for better visual hierarchy.

The plotThe code














p1 / (p2 | p3)






Alternative 4: Letter tags instead of Roman numerals
Using letter tags (A, B, C) makes subplots easier to reference in written explanations.

The plotThe code














((p1 / p2) | p3) + 
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = 'A')






Alternative 5: Inset in different position
Placing the inset in the bottom right instead of top left avoids covering the main data cluster.

The plotThe code














p3 + inset_element(p2,
                   left = 0.5,
                   bottom = 0.0,
                   right = 1.0,
                   top = 0.4)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
Animated graphics can make data stories more engaging and memorable compared to static charts. In this exercise, I learnt how to create animated visualisations using gganimate and plotly, while also practising data wrangling with tidyr and dplyr.
Before diving in, it’s worth asking, does animation actually add value here? For exploratory analysis, probably not. But for presentations, a well-placed animation can really help the audience connect with the data.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
Animated graphics can make data stories more engaging and memorable compared to static charts. In this exercise, I learnt how to create animated visualisations using gganimate and plotly, while also practising data wrangling with tidyr and dplyr.
Before diving in, it’s worth asking, does animation actually add value here? For exploratory analysis, probably not. But for presentations, a well-placed animation can really help the audience connect with the data.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
Reflections
This was my first time making charts interactive in R. The ggiraph package was surprisingly straightforward — just swap geom_point() for geom_point_interactive() and wrap it in girafe().
The most interesting part was the coordinated multiple views where hovering on one chart highlights the same student in another chart. This is really useful for spotting patterns across different variables at the same time.
I found plotly easier to use than ggiraph since ggplotly() can just wrap an existing ggplot chart. The crosstalk + DT combination was also very cool, being able to filter a chart by clicking on a table row is something I can see being very useful in real dashboards.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
Getting Started

Loading packages

pacman::p_load(readxl, gifski, gapminder,
               plotly, gganimate, tidyverse)



Importing data
This exercise uses a different dataset — GlobalPopulation.xls instead of Exam_data.csv.

col <- c("Country", "Continent")
globalPop <- read_xls("GlobalPopulation.xls",
                      sheet="Data") %>%
  mutate(across(all_of(col), as.factor)) %>%
  mutate(Year = as.integer(Year))

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
Animated Data Visualisation — gganimate

Static bubble plot first
Always good to build the static version before animating — helps check the layout looks right.

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Old, y = Young, 
                      size = Population, 
                      colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, 
             show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(title = 'Year: {frame_time}', 
       x = '% Aged', 
       y = '% Young')






Animated bubble plot
Adding transition_time() animates the chart across years. ease_aes('linear') makes the movement smooth and consistent.

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Old, y = Young, 
                      size = Population, 
                      colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, 
             show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(title = 'Year: {frame_time}', 
       x = '% Aged', 
       y = '% Young') +
  transition_time(Year) +       
  ease_aes('linear')

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
Animated Data Visualisation — plotly

Using ggplotly()
This converts the ggplot animation into an interactive plotly chart with a play/pause button and a year slider — much more user-friendly for presentations.

The plotThe code chunk











gg <- ggplot(globalPop, 
       aes(x = Old, 
           y = Young, 
           size = Population, 
           colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(aes(size = Population,
                 frame = Year),
             alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(x = '% Aged', 
       y = '% Young') + 
  theme(legend.position='none')

ggplotly(gg)






Using plot_ly()
Building the animated chart directly with plot_ly() — slightly different syntax but gives more control over the output.

The plotThe code chunk











bp <- globalPop %>%
  plot_ly(x = ~Old, 
          y = ~Young, 
          size = ~Population, 
          color = ~Continent,
          sizes = c(2, 100),
          frame = ~Year, 
          text = ~Country, 
          hoverinfo = "text",
          type = 'scatter',
          mode = 'markers') %>%
  layout(showlegend = FALSE)
bp

title: Hands-on Exercise 4b: Visual Statistical Analysis
text:
ggstatsplot is really useful because it combines the chart and the statistical test result in one — something that would normally take two separate steps in R. The ggbetweenstats() function was the most interesting since it automatically runs the right test depending on the number of groups and shows the pairwise comparisons directly on the chart.
The Bayes Factor in gghistostats() was new to me — it gives a more intuitive way to interpret evidence compared to just a p-value.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4b: Visual Statistical Analysis
text:
ggstatsplot is really useful because it combines the chart and the statistical test result in one — something that would normally take two separate steps in R. The ggbetweenstats() function was the most interesting since it automatically runs the right test depending on the number of groups and shows the pairwise comparisons directly on the chart.
The Bayes Factor in gghistostats() was new to me — it gives a more intuitive way to interpret evidence compared to just a p-value.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4b: Visual Statistical Analysis
text:
Getting Started

Loading packages

pacman::p_load(ggstatsplot, tidyverse)



Importing data

exam <- read_csv("Exam_data.csv")

title: Hands-on Exercise 4b: Visual Statistical Analysis
text:
One-sample test: gghistostats()
Testing whether the mean English score is significantly different from 60.

The plotThe code chunk














set.seed(1234)

gghistostats(
  data = exam,
  x = ENGLISH,
  type = "bayes",
  test.value = 60,
  xlab = "English scores"
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 4b: Visual Statistical Analysis
text:
Two-sample mean test: ggbetweenstats()
Comparing Maths scores between genders using a non-parametric test.

The plotThe code chunk














ggbetweenstats(
  data = exam,
  x = GENDER,
  y = MATHS,
  type = "np",
  messages = FALSE
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 4b: Visual Statistical Analysis
text:
Oneway ANOVA Test: ggbetweenstats()
Comparing English scores across different races with pairwise comparisons.

The plotThe code chunk














ggbetweenstats(
  data = exam,
  x = RACE,
  y = ENGLISH,
  type = "p",
  mean.ci = TRUE,
  pairwise.comparisons = TRUE,
  pairwise.display = "s",
  p.adjust.method = "fdr",
  messages = FALSE
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 4b: Visual Statistical Analysis
text:
Significant Test of Correlation: ggscatterstats()
Testing whether there is a significant correlation between Maths and English scores.

The plotThe code chunk














ggscatterstats(
  data = exam,
  x = MATHS,
  y = ENGLISH,
  marginal = FALSE
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 4b: Visual Statistical Analysis
text:
Significant Test of Association: ggbarstats()
Testing the association between Maths results (pass/fail) and gender.

The plotThe code chunk














exam1 <- exam %>%
  mutate(MATHS_bins =
           cut(MATHS,
               breaks = c(0,60,75,85,100))
)

ggbarstats(exam1,
           x = MATHS_bins,
           y = GENDER)

title: Hands-on Exercise 4d: Building Funnel Plot with R
text: A funnel plot is used for fair comparison between groups of different sizes. In this exercise I used COVID-19 data from Jakarta to compare cumulative fatality rates across sub-districts.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4d: Building Funnel Plot with R
text: A funnel plot is used for fair comparison between groups of different sizes. In this exercise I used COVID-19 data from Jakarta to compare cumulative fatality rates across sub-districts.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4d: Building Funnel Plot with R
text:
Reflections
Funnel plots were completely new to me. The key insight is that comparing raw rates between areas of different sizes is unfair, a small sub-district with 2 deaths out of 10 cases looks much worse than a large one with 200 deaths out of 5000 cases, even if the true rate is similar.
The funnel shape comes from the fact that uncertainty decreases as sample size grows, small areas sit further out in the funnel, and outliers outside the 95% or 99% limits are the ones worth investigating.
Building the ggplot2 version from scratch was challenging but helped me understand exactly how the confidence limits are calculated.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4d: Building Funnel Plot with R
text:
Getting Started

Loading packages

pacman::p_load(tidyverse, FunnelPlotR, plotly, knitr)



Importing data

covid19 <- read_csv("COVID-19_DKI_Jakarta.csv") %>%
  mutate_if(is.character, as.factor)

title: Hands-on Exercise 4d: Building Funnel Plot with R
text:
FunnelPlotR methods

Basic funnel plot

The plotThe code chunk











A funnel plot object with 267 points of which 0 are outliers. 
Plot is adjusted for overdispersion. 





funnel_plot(
  .data = covid19,
  numerator = Positive,
  denominator = Death,
  group = `Sub-district`
)






Makeover 1 — using proportions

The plotThe code chunk











A funnel plot object with 267 points of which 7 are outliers. 
Plot is adjusted for overdispersion. 





funnel_plot(
  .data = covid19,
  numerator = Death,
  denominator = Positive,
  group = `Sub-district`,
  data_type = "PR",
  xrange = c(0, 6500),
  yrange = c(0, 0.05)
)






Makeover 2 — with proper labels

The plotThe code chunk











A funnel plot object with 267 points of which 7 are outliers. 
Plot is adjusted for overdispersion. 





funnel_plot(
  .data = covid19,
  numerator = Death,
  denominator = Positive,
  group = `Sub-district`,
  data_type = "PR",
  xrange = c(0, 6500),
  yrange = c(0, 0.05),
  label = NA,
  title = "Cumulative COVID-19 Fatality Rate by Cumulative Total Number of COVID-19 Positive Cases",
  x_label = "Cumulative COVID-19 Positive Cases",
  y_label = "Cumulative Fatality Rate"
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 4d: Building Funnel Plot with R
text:
Funnel Plot using ggplot2

Computing derived fields

df <- covid19 %>%
  mutate(rate = Death / Positive) %>%
  mutate(rate.se = sqrt((rate*(1-rate)) / (Positive))) %>%
  filter(rate > 0)

fit.mean <- weighted.mean(df$rate, 1/df$rate.se^2)



Calculate 95% and 99.9% confidence limits

number.seq <- seq(1, max(df$Positive), 1)
number.ll95 <- fit.mean - 1.96 * sqrt((fit.mean*(1-fit.mean)) / (number.seq))
number.ul95 <- fit.mean + 1.96 * sqrt((fit.mean*(1-fit.mean)) / (number.seq))
number.ll999 <- fit.mean - 3.29 * sqrt((fit.mean*(1-fit.mean)) / (number.seq))
number.ul999 <- fit.mean + 3.29 * sqrt((fit.mean*(1-fit.mean)) / (number.seq))
dfCI <- data.frame(number.ll95, number.ul95,
                   number.ll999, number.ul999,
                   number.seq, fit.mean)



Static funnel plot

The plotThe code chunk














p <- ggplot(df, aes(x = Positive, y = rate)) +
  geom_point(aes(label=`Sub-district`),
             alpha=0.4,
             size=0.9) +
  geom_line(data = dfCI,
            aes(x = number.seq,
                y = number.ll95),
            linewidth = 0.4,
            colour = "grey40",
            linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_line(data = dfCI,
            aes(x = number.seq,
                y = number.ul95),
            linewidth = 0.4,
            colour = "grey40",
            linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_line(data = dfCI,
            aes(x = number.seq,
                y = number.ll999),
            linewidth = 0.4,
            colour = "grey40") +
  geom_line(data = dfCI,
            aes(x = number.seq,
                y = number.ul999),
            linewidth = 0.4,
            colour = "grey40") +
  geom_hline(data = dfCI,
             aes(yintercept = fit.mean),
             linewidth = 0.4,
             colour = "grey40") +
  coord_cartesian(ylim=c(0,0.05)) +
  ggtitle("Cumulative Fatality Rate by Cumulative Number of COVID-19 Cases") +
  xlab("Cumulative Number of COVID-19 Cases") +
  ylab("Cumulative Fatality Rate") +
  theme_light() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(size=12))
p






Interactive funnel plot

The plotThe code chunk











fp_ggplotly <- ggplotly(p,
  tooltip = c("label",
              "x",
              "y"))
fp_ggplotly

title: In-class Exercise 3
text:
In-Class Exercise 3 builds on previous Tableau exercises by creating two separate dashboards using different datasets.
The first dashboard uses the Superstore dataset to analyse sales and profit performance across product sub-categories and customers. The second uses SingStat’s June 2025 population data to visualise the age and gender distribution of Singapore residents across three planning areas.

title: In-class Exercise 3
text:
In-Class Exercise 3 builds on previous Tableau exercises by creating two separate dashboards using different datasets.
The first dashboard uses the Superstore dataset to analyse sales and profit performance across product sub-categories and customers. The second uses SingStat’s June 2025 population data to visualise the age and gender distribution of Singapore residents across three planning areas.

title: In-class Exercise 3
text:
Dashboard 1: SuperStore Sales Report
The interactive dashboard can be viewed on Tableau Public at the link below:
👉 View Dashboard on Tableau Public



SuperStore Sales Report


This dashboard contains 5 views:

Sales versus Profit Quadrant by State — a bubble scatter plot mapping each state by sales and profit percentile. Bubble size encodes sales volume and colour encodes profit (blue = profitable, orange = loss-making).
SuperStore Sales Report — a general overview of overall sales performance across the dataset.
SubCategory and Profit — a Pareto chart ranking sub-categories by profit, with a cumulative line showing that Copiers, Phones, and Accessories drive the majority of total profit.
Customer ID and Profit — customers ranked by individual profit contribution, with a running cumulative sum and average reference line.
Pareto Chart by Customer ID — The x-axis represents the cumulative percentage of customers, while the y-axis represents the cumulative percentage of total profit. The two dashed reference lines intersect at the 20% customer mark and 80% profit mark, confirming the Pareto principle. The curve rises steeply in the early portion before gradually levelling off, indicating that a small segment of customers generates a disproportionately large share of profit.




SubCategory and Profit — Pareto Chart





Customer ID and Profit





Pareto Chart by Customer ID

title: In-class Exercise 3
section: Dashboard 2: Distribution by Age and Region
text:
Dashboard 2: Distribution by Age and Region
The interactive dashboard can be viewed on Tableau Public at the link below:
👉 View Dashboard on Tableau Public
Data source: respopagesexfa2025.csv from SingStat — Singapore Residents by Planning Area/Subzone, Age Group, Sex and Floor Area of Residence, June 2025.



Distribution by Age and Region — Ang Mo Kio, Tampines, Punggol


The side-by-side population pyramids compare three planning areas:

Ang Mo Kio has a concentration in the middle-to-older age bands (40–74), reflecting a mature, established residential town.
Tampines shows a broad working-age base (30–69), consistent with a large, well-developed HDB estate.
Punggol has a distinctly younger profile — the largest bars sit in the 25–44 range with a strong base of young children, reflecting its newer development attracting young families.

Pink bars represent Females and blue bars represent Males.
Pareto Charts were a key new technique in this exercise. By combining a sorted bar chart with a cumulative percentage line, I could quickly identify which sub-categories and customers drive the most profit. The 80/20 pattern appeared clearly in both the sub-category and customer-level analyses.
Population Pyramids required a different approach — splitting the dataset by sex and mirroring bars on opposite sides of a central axis. Comparing three planning areas side-by-side made demographic differences immediately visible, something a table of numbers could not achieve as effectively.
